narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've put together a respectable academic profile for law school admission. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated a 58.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your school decile rankings from earlier years: specifically, your Year 3 decile of 2—well below the average of 5.53—stood out as the top factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure, suggesting relatively weaker performance compared to peers at that stage. Nearly as influential was your Year 1 decile of 1, far below the average of 5.71, which reinforced the model's concern about foundational academic struggles. On a more positive note, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward bar exam success by demonstrating strong standardized test ability. Overall, these early decile rankings carried the most weight, outweighing your other strengths and setting you apart from typical admitted students who tend to rank higher in school deciles throughout. While your full-time status and background align with successful applicants, the model saw your lower early rankings as a red flag for bar passage risk. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through community college or targeted prep that addresses those early weaknesses. Strengthening your LSAT further or gaining relevant experience could also help in reapplying. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on consistent grade improvement—your path isn't closed, just needs targeted refinement.
